terms.def: Volume-Weighted Average Price; a common execution benchmark — trade in proportion to market volume over the day.
Time-Weighted Average Price; slice the order evenly over time intervals regardless of market volume.
Difference between paper portfolio return (decision price) and actual portfolio return; the total execution cost.
Price moves against the order as it is filled; scales roughly with √(order size / ADV).
% of market volume that the algorithm trades; higher = faster execution but more impact.
Average Daily Volume; the normalising factor for order size — a 1% ADV order is small; 30% ADV is very large.

eli3: Execution strategy is how you actually buy or sell a large position without moving the market against yourself. If you need to buy $100M of a stock that trades $10M/day, you can't do it in one trade — you'll drive the price up. Execution algorithms break the order into smaller pieces over time to minimise market impact (the cost of moving the price by trading).

The main trade-off is: trade faster and pay more market impact (but less timing risk — price may move against you if you wait), or trade slowly and pay less market impact (but more timing risk). VWAP and TWAP are common benchmark algorithms. Arrival price (Implementation Shortfall) algorithms focus on minimising the gap between the decision price and the average execution price.

Market impact is the biggest hidden cost in asset management — larger than explicit commissions for large funds. A $10B fund turning over 100% per year might pay 5-15bps in commissions but 20-50bps in market impact. Understanding and minimising execution costs is a core competency for institutional traders.

whenWhy.title: VWAP, TWAP, and participation rate algorithms
whenWhy.body: Time-Weighted Average Price (TWAP): split the order equally over time intervals (e.g., execute 1/8 of the order every hour over 8 hours). Simple but ignores intraday volume patterns. Use case: when timing risk is symmetric (no directional view on short-term price movement) and volume profile is unknown. Volume-Weighted Average Price (VWAP): trade in proportion to the historical volume profile. If 15% of daily volume trades in the first 30 minutes, execute 15% of the order in that window. VWAP is the most common equity execution benchmark — tracks the day's average price weighted by volume. Use case: minimise execution cost relative to the market's average price; reduces market impact vs. TWAP by avoiding times of low liquidity. Participation rate: trade at a fixed percentage of market volume (e.g., 20% of every trade in the market). Ensures the order is filled in proportion to available liquidity. Use case: large orders in thin markets where over-participation risks excessive impact. All three are 'passive' algorithms — they accept the prevailing price at each interval without trying to predict short-term price movements.

whenWhy.title: Implementation shortfall and arrival price
whenWhy.body: Implementation Shortfall (IS) — Perold (1988): IS = (Execution price - Decision price) × Shares executed + Opportunity cost of unexecuted shares. IS decomposes into: (1) Market impact: price moved against you because you were trading; (2) Timing cost: price moved during the execution period due to market-wide movement; (3) Delay cost: time between decision and start of execution; (4) Opportunity cost: shares not executed (in case of partial fill). IS algorithms minimise total IS by: (a) Trading faster when price is moving against you (urgency increases); (b) Trading slower when price moves in your favour (timing works for you). Almgren-Chriss model: optimal IS schedule x*(t) = X × cosh(κ(T-t)) / cosh(κT), where κ = √(λ/η), λ = risk aversion, η = market impact coefficient. The schedule front-loads execution when risk aversion (λ) is high. Arrival price benchmark: simpler version — benchmark against the price at order arrival. IS relative to arrival price = (average fill price - arrival price). This is now the industry standard performance metric for active funds.

whenWhy.title: Dark pools and alternative execution venues
whenWhy.body: Execution venues: (1) Exchange (lit market): transparent order book, best price discovery, full regulatory oversight. High market impact visibility — other traders see your order. (2) Dark pools (ATS in the US, MTF in Europe): no pre-trade transparency; orders match at midpoint. Advantages: zero market impact from pre-trade information leakage; price improvement (midpoint vs. bid-ask spread). Disadvantages: adverse selection — counterparties in dark pools are often informed traders or high-frequency traders; fill uncertainty (dark pools don't guarantee execution); toxicity (some dark pools route orders to HFT firms). (3) Systematic internalisers (SI): banks matching client orders internally. Reduce exchange fees but add counterparty risk. (4) Direct market access (DMA) / algorithmic direct: fund sends orders directly through exchange without broker intermediation. (5) Request for Quote (RFQ): for bonds, FX, and derivatives — send RFQ to multiple dealers simultaneously; take best price. Smart Order Routing (SOR): algorithms route order fragments across multiple venues to minimise total cost. Essential for large institutional orders.

deepDiveIntro: Market impact model: linear temporary impact + permanent impact. Price during execution: S(t) = S(0) + η × v(t) (temporary, linear in trading rate v) + γ × X × (permanent, linear in total volume X). Market impact cost = η × ∫₀ᵀ v(t)² dt + γX². Minimising: the optimal schedule (ignoring timing risk) is linear: v*(t) = X/T — trade at constant rate. Adding risk aversion (variance of mark-to-market): the optimal schedule becomes front-loaded. Almgren-Chriss optimal execution: minimise E[cost] + λ × Var[cost]. This is the standard mathematical framework underlying modern execution algorithms. Parameters estimated from intraday transaction data: η (temporary impact) and γ (permanent impact) vary by stock liquidity (measured by ADV: average daily volume).

deepDive.heading: Transaction Cost Analysis (TCA)
deepDive.body: Transaction Cost Analysis: systematic post-trade measurement of execution quality. TCA measures: (1) Explicit costs: commissions, exchange fees, taxes (stamp duty, FTT); (2) Implicit costs: market impact (arrival price slippage), bid-ask spread, timing costs; (3) Implementation Shortfall: total explicit + implicit costs vs. decision price. TCA reporting for asset managers: required by MiFID II (Europe) for best execution demonstration. Must report execution quality quarterly, show that execution venues and brokers provide competitive pricing. Benchmark comparisons: IS vs. arrival price; VWAP performance; peer comparisons using aggregated data from TCA providers (Abel Noser, ConvergEx, ITG, Bloomberg). TCA for fixed income: bond markets are OTC (over-the-counter), no centralised exchange. TCA relies on TRACE data (US), FINRA reporting — less complete than equity TCA. Bid-ask spreads dominate; market impact harder to measure. Electronic trading (MarketAxess, Tradeweb) has improved fixed income TCA by creating observable prices. [1]

deepDive.heading: Pre-Trade Analysis and Order Scheduling
deepDive.body: Before execution: pre-trade analysis estimates expected costs and selects optimal algorithm. Pre-trade model: E[IS] = f(order size, stock liquidity, urgency, market conditions). Inputs: (1) Order size as % of ADV: >5% ADV = large order requiring careful scheduling; >20% ADV = multi-day execution; (2) Volatility: high vol = higher urgency (timing risk); (3) Spread: wide spread stocks require different approach (midpoint crossing in dark pool); (4) Market conditions: high VIX = reduce participation rate to avoid adverse impact. Intraday volume profiles: stock volume follows a U-shaped pattern (high at open and close, low midday). VWAP algorithms front-load at open and back-load at close. Pre-trade alpha: if the fund has a short-horizon alpha signal (e.g., the stock will rise 30bps in the next hour based on news flow), the urgency increases and IS algorithm should trade faster. Institutional alpha decay: for most fundamental strategies, alpha has an hour-to-days decay; execution within 1-2 days is sufficient. For high-frequency signals, execution within minutes is critical. [2]

deepDive.heading: High-Frequency Trading and Market Structure
deepDive.body: High-frequency trading (HFT) impact on institutional execution: (1) Adverse selection: HFT firms co-locate at exchanges, exploit microsecond speed advantage to 'detect' large institutional orders and trade ahead (front-running in the loosest sense). IEX exchange (introduced speed bump of 350μs) reduced HFT adverse selection; (2) Market making: HFT firms provide liquidity by continuously quoting bid and ask. This narrows spreads for small orders but withdraws in crises (liquidity evaporates); (3) Quote stuffing: some HFT strategies flood the market with rapid order submissions and cancellations — regulators have intervened with order-to-trade ratios; (4) Flash crashes: May 6, 2010 — S&P 500 fell 9% in minutes and recovered; June 2010 circuit breakers introduced. Institutional response to HFT: (a) Route large orders to dark pools to avoid HFT detection; (b) Randomise execution timing and size; (c) Use broker-operated dark pools (known counterparties); (d) TWAP over random intervals instead of deterministic schedule. MiFID II (2018): required best execution reporting, transaction reporting, and restricted certain HFT practices. [1]

takeaways: VWAP: execute in proportion to historical volume profile — standard equity benchmark; TWAP: equal-time intervals. [1]
Implementation Shortfall (IS) = market impact + timing cost + delay cost + opportunity cost vs. decision price. [1]
Almgren-Chriss optimal execution: minimise E[cost] + λ × Var[cost]; front-loads when risk aversion is high. [2]
Dark pools: mid-price execution, no pre-trade transparency; reduces information leakage but risk of adverse selection. [1]
TCA required by MiFID II; measures explicit + implicit costs; fixed income TCA harder (OTC market, TRACE data). [1]

edgeCases.q: How do you execute a large order in an illiquid stock without revealing your intent?
edgeCases.a: Stealth execution for large illiquid orders: (1) Fragmentation: split into many small orders across multiple venues and times. No single trade is large enough to reveal size. Use random order sizes and timing intervals to prevent detection of pattern; (2) Dark pools: route to multiple dark pools simultaneously. Midpoint crossing avoids market impact from lit markets. But: dark pool fill rates are low for illiquid stocks (20-40% fill rate typical); (3) Natural counterparty search: use broker network to find natural sellers (buyside firms with the opposite intent). Block trading desks (e.g., Liquidnet) facilitate large block trades at midpoint. More price-efficient than algorithmic fragmentation; (4) Block auctions (Cboe periodic auctions, BATSTrade): scheduled block auctions attract natural counterparties at a single price. Lower information leakage than lit markets; (5) Reduce order size: if the stock is too illiquid, reduce position target. Force-feeding a large order into an illiquid stock creates market impact that destroys the alpha premise; (6) Time the execution: avoid earnings, index reconstitutions, sector events when information asymmetry is highest. Execute over days/weeks using VWAP with very low participation rate (5% of ADV). Key principle: when in doubt, trade less. Preserving alpha by waiting is usually better than trading into impact.

edgeCases.q: What is the difference between market impact and adverse selection in execution costs?
edgeCases.a: Market impact: price moves against you because YOUR order caused it. You are buying; the act of buying pushes the price up. Mechanism: (1) Temporary impact: price temporarily rises while you're buying, then partially reverses when you stop; (2) Permanent impact: some price rise persists because your buying signals information to the market. Adverse selection: price moves against you because the OTHER side knew something you didn't. You are selling; the buyer knows the stock is about to rise. You get filled on your sell order, then the price jumps — you sold to someone with better information. Adverse selection is endemic in dark pools where HFT firms participate. How to distinguish: (1) Price reversion: if the price partially reverts after your order completes → temporary market impact dominated; (2) No reversion → permanent impact or adverse selection; (3) Pre-trade vs. post-trade analysis: adverse selection shows up as price moving against you in the minutes AFTER the trade completes (not during). Post-trade reversion analysis is the standard way to disentangle. Mitigation: adverse selection can be reduced by trading with natural counterparties (other buy-side firms, not HFTs), using anonymous block-crossing networks, and avoiding venues with known HFT participation. Market impact can be reduced by slower trading with lower participation rate.

sources.label: Perold, A.F. (1988) — The Implementation Shortfall, JPM
Almgren, R. & Chriss, N. (2001) — Optimal Execution of Portfolio Transactions, Journal of Risk
